Sol: Our Sun, a G2V main-sequence star and the center of the Solar System. At 4.6 billion years old, it provides the benchmark for stellar luminosity and mass.
Alpheratz: The brightest star in Andromeda and formerly shared with Pegasus, a chemically peculiar B9 star with an overabundance of manganese and mercury. At 30 parsecs distant, it marks the northeast corner of the Great Square of Pegasus.
Caph: A Delta Scuti variable and one of the nearest F-type subgiant/giant stars at 17 parsecs. Beta Cassiopeiae forms the westernmost point of the distinctive W-shape of Cassiopeia.
Algenib: A Beta Cephei variable B2 subgiant in Pegasus, pulsating with a period of about 3.6 hours. It marks the southeastern corner of the Great Square of Pegasus at 120 parsecs.
Groombridge 34: A nearby red dwarf binary system just 3.6 parsecs away in Andromeda, notable as one of the closest star systems to the Sun. Both components are flare stars prone to sudden brightness increases.
Citadelle: A K-type star in Pisces at 159 parsecs, named through the IAU's 2019 NameExoWorlds campaign by Cote d'Ivoire. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Ankaa: Alpha Phoenicis, the brightest star in Phoenix and a K0 giant at 26 parsecs. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the phoenix,' marking the mythical bird's breast.
Felixvarela: A K5 dwarf in Cetus at 36 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Cuba in honor of the philosopher Felix Varela. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Fulu: Zeta Cassiopeiae, a B2 subgiant at 182 parsecs that is a slowly pulsating B-type variable. Its name derives from a Chinese asterism meaning 'the Auxiliary Road.'
Schedar: Alpha Cassiopeiae, a K0 giant at 70 parsecs that rivals Caph as the brightest star in the W of Cassiopeia. It is a suspected variable with a slightly orange hue from its cool photosphere.
Diphda: Beta Ceti, the brightest star in Cetus and a K0 giant at 30 parsecs. Despite its Beta designation, it outshines Alpha Ceti (Menkar), and its name means 'the second frog' in Arabic.
Cocibolca: A G5 dwarf in Sculptor at 32 parsecs, named by Nicaragua through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign after Lake Cocibolca. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Shimu: Zeta Andromedae, a K1 giant at 58 parsecs that is a chromospherically active RS Canum Venaticorum variable. Its Chinese-derived name references a historical asterism.
96 G. Psc: A nearby K2 dwarf in Pisces at only 7.5 parsecs, cataloged by Gould. It is a relatively Sun-like star and one of the closer solar neighborhood members.
Achird: Eta Cassiopeiae, a nearby G0 dwarf with a faint K-type companion at just 6 parsecs. The primary is remarkably Sun-like and the pair form a wide visual binary with a 480-year orbital period.
Van Maanen's Star: The closest known solitary white dwarf to the Sun at just 4.3 parsecs in Pisces. Discovered by Adriaan van Maanen in 1917, it was the third white dwarf identified and has a helium-rich atmosphere polluted by accreted metals.
Castula: Upsilon-2 Cassiopeiae, a G8 giant at 61 parsecs whose name derives from a Latin word for a small fortress. It lies within the W-shaped figure of Cassiopeia.
Cih: Gamma Cassiopeiae, a hot B0 star at 168 parsecs and the prototype of a class of rapidly rotating Be stars that eject matter into circumstellar disks. Its erratic brightness variations between magnitudes 1.6 and 3.0 make it a fascinating variable.
Kui: Eta Andromedae, a G8 giant at 70 parsecs whose name comes from a Chinese lunar mansion. It is a spectroscopic binary with a period of about 115 days.
Nenque: A G3 subgiant in Phoenix at 41 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Costa Rica. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Wurren: Zeta Phoenicis, an eclipsing binary of Algol type with B6 and B0 components at 92 parsecs. It was the first eclipsing binary discovered in the southern hemisphere.
Mirach: Beta Andromedae, a red M0 giant at 61 parsecs that serves as a key guide star for locating the Andromeda Galaxy. Its warm reddish glow and magnitude 2.1 make it easily visible to the naked eye.
Emiw: A K0 dwarf in Tucana at 35 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet in a relatively close orbit.
Revati: Zeta Piscium A, an A7 subgiant at 53 parsecs in Pisces. Its name comes from a Hindu lunar mansion (nakshatra) associated with prosperity and wealth.
Revati B: The F7 dwarf companion to Zeta Piscium A, forming a wide optical double. At about 94 parsecs, it may be at a different distance than Revati A.
Adhil: Xi Andromedae, a K0 giant at 66 parsecs near the border of Andromeda. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the train of a garment,' referencing its position in the constellation figure.
Belenos: An F8 star in Pisces at 45 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Switzerland after the Celtic deity of light. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Ruchbah: Delta Cassiopeiae, an A5 spectroscopic binary at 30 parsecs forming part of the famous W asterism. It is a mildly variable star whose name means 'the knee' in Arabic.
Alpherg: Eta Piscium, the brightest star in Pisces, a G8 giant at 107 parsecs. Its name likely derives from the Arabic for 'the covering,' and it is a binary system.
Titawin: Upsilon Andromedae, a nearby F8 dwarf at 13 parsecs that hosts one of the first known multi-planet extrasolar systems. At least four giant planets orbit this Sun-like star.
Achernar: Alpha Eridani, the brightest star in Eridanus and the ninth brightest in the sky at magnitude 0.45. A rapidly rotating B3 star at 43 parsecs, it is so oblate that its equatorial diameter is 50% larger than its polar diameter.
Nembus: 51 Andromedae, a K3 giant at 54 parsecs in northern Andromeda. Its name appears in historical star catalogs, and it lies near the border with Perseus.
p Eridani: A nearby K0 dwarf binary system at 7.8 parsecs in Eridanus. Both components are slightly cooler than the Sun and form a close pair resolvable in small telescopes.
Torcular: Omicron Piscium, a K0 giant at 86 parsecs in Pisces. Its Latin name means 'wine press,' referencing the historical depiction of the star in Renaissance atlases.
Baten Kaitos: Zeta Ceti, a K2 giant at 72 parsecs whose Arabic name means 'the belly of the sea monster.' It marks the body of the constellation Cetus.
Mothallah: Alpha Trianguli, an F6 subgiant at 19 parsecs and the second brightest star in the compact constellation Triangulum. Its name means 'the triangle' in Arabic.
Mesarthim: Gamma Arietis, an A1 chemically peculiar star at 50 parsecs that was one of the first double stars discovered by telescope (by Robert Hooke in 1664). Its components are nearly equal in brightness.
Segin: Epsilon Cassiopeiae, a B2 variable star at 126 parsecs forming the leftmost point of Cassiopeia's W. It is a blue subgiant nearing the end of its hydrogen-burning phase.
Sheratan: Beta Arietis, an A5 spectroscopic binary at 18 parsecs and the second brightest star in Aries. Its name means 'the two signs,' referencing the vernal equinox that once lay nearby.
Alrescha: Alpha Piscium, the knot star where the two cords of Pisces meet, an A2 binary at 46 parsecs. The two nearly equal components orbit each other with a period of about 720 years.
Almach: Gamma Andromedae, a stunning multiple star system at 120 parsecs famous for its color contrast -- a golden K giant primary with a blue B8 companion. It is one of the finest double stars visible in amateur telescopes.
Hamal: Alpha Arietis, the brightest star in Aries and a K2 giant at 20 parsecs. Around 2000 BCE, it marked the vernal equinox, making it one of the most historically significant stars in navigation and astrology.
Deltoton: Delta Trianguli, a nearby G0 dwarf at 11 parsecs that closely resembles the Sun. It is a spectroscopic binary with a faint M-dwarf companion in a 10-day orbit.
Mira: Omicron Ceti, the prototype of an entire class of pulsating red giant variable stars. Its dramatic brightness changes from magnitude 2 to 10 over a 332-day period made it the first non-supernova variable star discovered, by David Fabricius in 1596.
Polaris: Alpha Ursae Minoris, the current North Star and the brightest Cepheid variable, an F7 supergiant at 133 parsecs. Its position less than a degree from the north celestial pole has made it indispensable for navigation for centuries.
268 G. Cet: A nearby K3 dwarf in Cetus at 7.2 parsecs, among the closest stars to the Sun. It is a quiet, slowly rotating star older than the Sun with a very low level of chromospheric activity.
Buna: A G0 star in Andromeda at 58 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Ethiopia after the coffee plant. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Kaffaljidhma: Gamma Ceti, an A3 dwarf at 24 parsecs whose elaborate Arabic name means 'the head of the whale.' It is a binary star with a faint F-type companion.
Koeia: A faint M0 red dwarf in Eridanus at 23 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. Despite its dim appearance at magnitude 10.2, it is notable for hosting a confirmed exoplanet.
Lilii Borea: 39 Arietis, a K1 giant at 53 parsecs named by the French astronomer Le Francais de Lalande. The name means 'the northern lily' and it was historically placed at the border of the now-defunct constellation Lilium.
Nushagak: A G5 star in Cassiopeia at 75 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by the United States after the Nushagak River in Alaska. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Bharani: 41 Arietis, a B8 dwarf at 51 parsecs named after the second nakshatra (lunar mansion) in Hindu astronomy. It is a fast-rotating star with a slightly variable brightness.
Miram: Eta Persei, a luminous K3 supergiant at 270 parsecs and one of the most luminous stars visible to the naked eye. It is a spectroscopic binary with a composite spectrum.
Angetenar: Tau-2 Eridani, a K0 giant at 57 parsecs in the River Eridanus. Its Arabic name means 'the bend of the river,' marking a turn in the winding constellation figure.
Azha: Eta Eridani, a K1 giant at 42 parsecs in Eridanus. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the breeding place,' possibly referencing an ostrich nest in ancient Arabian star lore.
Acamar: Theta-1 Eridani, a striking binary of two A-type stars at 49 parsecs. Before Achernar was cataloged, Acamar marked the southern end of the constellation Eridanus, and its name shares the same Arabic root meaning 'end of the river.'
Ayeyarwady: A G8/K0 subgiant in Eridanus at 135 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Myanmar after the Ayeyarwady River. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Menkar: Alpha Ceti, a cool M2 red giant at 76 parsecs that is the traditional 'head of the sea monster.' Despite its Alpha designation, it is actually the second brightest star in Cetus after Diphda.
Algol: Beta Persei, the most famous eclipsing binary star and the prototype of the Algol-type variables. At 28 parsecs, its regular dips from magnitude 2.1 to 3.4 every 2.87 days were known to ancient astronomers, earning it the Arabic name 'the Demon's Head.'
Misam: Kappa Persei, a K0 giant at 35 parsecs in Perseus. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the wrist,' marking the hero's arm in the constellation figure.
Botein: Delta Arietis, a K2 variable giant at 52 parsecs. Its Arabic name means 'the belly,' and it marks the body of the celestial ram.
Dalim: Alpha Fornacis, an F8 dwarf at 14 parsecs and the brightest star in the faint constellation Fornax. It is a binary system with a G7 dwarf companion in a 269-year orbit.
Zibal: Zeta Eridani, an A5m (metallic-line) star at 34 parsecs. Its name derives from Arabic and references a group of small ostriches in the traditional constellation figure.
82 G. Eri: A nearby G8 dwarf at only 6 parsecs in Eridanus, hosting at least three confirmed super-Earth exoplanets. It is one of the closest Sun-like stars known to harbor a planetary system.
Intan: A K3/K4 subgiant in Fornax at 46 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Malaysia. Its name means 'diamond' in Malay, and it hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Mirfak: Alpha Persei, a luminous F5 supergiant at 155 parsecs and the brightest star in Perseus. It anchors a conspicuous moving group of young blue stars called the Alpha Persei Cluster.
Ran: Epsilon Eridani, one of the nearest Sun-like stars at just 3.2 parsecs with a confirmed Jupiter-mass exoplanet and a dusty debris disk. Named after the Norse sea goddess, it has long been a prime target for SETI observations.
Tupi: An F8/G0 dwarf in Reticulum at 34 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Brazil after the indigenous Tupi people. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Rana: Delta Eridani, a K0 subgiant at just 9 parsecs that is evolving off the main sequence. Its Latin name means 'frog,' and it is one of the closer evolved stars to the Sun.
Atik: Omicron Persei, a massive B1 giant at 344 parsecs and an eclipsing spectroscopic binary. It is a hot, luminous star embedded in a region of faint nebulosity.
Celaeno: A B7 subgiant member of the Pleiades cluster in Taurus at 116 parsecs. Named for one of the seven daughters of Atlas in Greek mythology, it is among the fainter of the Pleiades visible to the naked eye.
Electra: A B6 giant and one of the brighter members of the Pleiades at 124 parsecs. Named for a daughter of Atlas, it is a rapidly rotating star surrounded by a faint gaseous disk.
Taygeta: A B6 dwarf in the Pleiades at 125 parsecs, named for a daughter of Atlas in Greek mythology. It is a spectroscopic binary with a faint companion.
Maia: A B8 giant in the Pleiades at 118 parsecs, once believed to be the prototype of a class of variable stars (Maia variables), though this classification remains debated. Named for the eldest of the Pleiades in Greek myth.
Asterope: A B8 dwarf in the Pleiades at 114 parsecs, one of the fainter named members of the cluster. It forms a visual double with Asterope B (22 Tauri).
Merope: A B6 subgiant in the Pleiades at 117 parsecs, surrounded by a reflection nebula (NGC 1435) illuminated by its blue light. In myth, Merope was the only Pleiad to marry a mortal, sometimes cited as the reason she appears faintest.
Alcyone: The brightest member of the Pleiades at magnitude 2.85, a B7 giant at 124 parsecs. It is an eclipsing binary surrounded by a gaseous shell, and in ancient times served as a reference star for various calendar systems.
Atlas: A B8 giant in the Pleiades at 117 parsecs, named for the Titan father of the seven sisters. It is a spectroscopic binary and one of the brighter members of the cluster.
Pleione: A rapidly rotating B7 emission-line star in the Pleiades at 117 parsecs, named for the mother of the seven sisters. It periodically ejects shells of gas, causing its brightness to vary unpredictably.
Zaurak: Gamma Eridani, a cool M1 giant at 62 parsecs with a distinctly reddish hue. Its name derives from Arabic and it marks a notable bend in the long constellation of Eridanus.
Menkib: Xi Persei, one of the hottest naked-eye stars visible, an O7.5 supergiant at 382 parsecs. It is a powerful source of ultraviolet radiation that ionizes the surrounding California Nebula.
Beid: Omicron-1 Eridani, an F2 giant at 37 parsecs that forms a naked-eye pair with Keid (Omicron-2 Eridani). Its name means 'the egg' in Arabic.
Rhombus: Alpha Reticuli, the brightest star in the small southern constellation Reticulum at 50 parsecs. It is a K0 giant whose constellation represents the reticle eyepiece of a telescope.
Keid: Omicron-2 Eridani, a remarkable triple system at just 5 parsecs containing a K1 dwarf, a white dwarf, and a red dwarf. The white dwarf component was the first to have its spectrum analyzed, confirming these objects as a distinct stellar class.
Prima Hyadum: Gamma Tauri, a G8 giant at 50 parsecs and the traditional 'first star of the Hyades' cluster. It marks the vertex of the V-shaped Hyades asterism that forms the face of Taurus.
Secunda Hyadum: Delta Tauri, the 'second star of the Hyades,' a G8 giant at 48 parsecs. Together with Prima Hyadum, it defines the face of the bull in Taurus.
Beemim: 43 Eridani, a K4 giant at 91 parsecs in the southern reaches of the constellation Eridanus. Its name may derive from Arabic star lore.
Ain: Epsilon Tauri, a K0 giant at 45 parsecs in the Hyades cluster with a confirmed Jupiter-mass exoplanet. Its name means 'the eye' in Arabic, as it marks the eye of the bull.
Chamukuy: Theta-2 Tauri, an A7 giant at 46 parsecs in the Hyades cluster. Its Mayan name means 'a type of small bird,' given through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Mexico.
Hoggar: A K0 star in Taurus at 227 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Algeria after the Hoggar Mountains. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Theemin: Upsilon-2 Eridani, a G8 giant at 66 parsecs in the southern portion of Eridanus. Its name derives from Arabic for 'the two ostriches,' part of an ancient grouping.
Aldebaran: Alpha Tauri, the 'Eye of the Bull' and the 14th brightest star in the sky, a K5 giant at 20 parsecs. Despite appearing to lie within the Hyades cluster, it is actually only half the cluster's distance and hosts a candidate giant exoplanet.
Sceptrum: 53 Eridani, a K1 giant at 34 parsecs that was formerly the brightest star of the defunct constellation Sceptrum Brandenburgicum. It now lies within the boundaries of Eridanus.
Tabit: Pi-3 Orionis, a nearby F6 dwarf at just 8 parsecs and one of the closer Sun-like stars. It is a candidate for harboring terrestrial planets in its habitable zone.
Mouhoun: A K0 giant in Eridanus at 118 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Burkina Faso after the Mouhoun River. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Hassaleh: Iota Aurigae, a luminous K3 giant at 151 parsecs that is one of the 'Kids' asterism in Auriga. Its name means 'the ankle bone' in Arabic.
Almaaz: Epsilon Aurigae, one of the most mysterious eclipsing binaries known, an F0 supergiant at 654 parsecs. Its extraordinary 27-year eclipse cycle, where an unseen companion dims it for nearly two years, has puzzled astronomers since its discovery.
Saclateni: Zeta Aurigae, an eclipsing binary at 241 parsecs in Auriga consisting of a K4 supergiant and a B-type companion. When the B star passes behind the giant's extended atmosphere, it acts as a probe revealing atmospheric structure.
Haedus: Eta Aurigae, a B3 dwarf at 75 parsecs forming part of the 'Kids' triangle asterism in Auriga. Its name means 'the kid' (young goat) in Latin.
Cursa: Beta Eridani, an A3 giant at 27 parsecs located at the foot of Orion, where Eridanus begins. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the footstool of the central one.'
Mago: A K1 giant in Camelopardalis at 121 parsecs, hosting a confirmed exoplanet. Named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign, it is notable as one of the few planet-hosting giants in this large but dim constellation.
Kapteyn's Star: A nearby M0 red subdwarf at 3.9 parsecs in Pictor, discovered by Jacobus Kapteyn in 1898. It has the second-highest known proper motion of any star and belongs to the galactic halo population, giving it an unusually high velocity and low metallicity.
Rigel: Beta Orionis, the seventh brightest star in the sky and a blue B8 supergiant at 265 parsecs. Despite its Beta designation, it typically outshines Alpha Orionis (Betelgeuse), and at 120,000 times solar luminosity it is one of the most intrinsically luminous stars visible to the naked eye.
Capella: Alpha Aurigae, the sixth brightest star in the sky and a spectroscopic binary of two G-type giants at 13 parsecs. This close pair orbits every 104 days and is accompanied by a distant pair of red dwarfs, making it a quadruple system.
Bellatrix: Gamma Orionis, a hot B2 giant at 77 parsecs marking Orion's left shoulder. Its Latin name means 'the female warrior,' and it is one of the hottest stars easily visible to the naked eye.
Elnath: Beta Tauri, a B7 giant at 41 parsecs shared historically between Taurus and Auriga. It is the second brightest star in Taurus and is sometimes classified as a mercury-manganese chemically peculiar star.
Nihal: Beta Leporis, a G5 bright giant at 49 parsecs and the second brightest star in Lepus the Hare. Its name means 'the drinking camels' in Arabic.
Mintaka: Delta Orionis, the westernmost star of Orion's Belt, a massive O9.5 giant at 212 parsecs. It is a complex multiple system with an eclipsing binary at its core, and it lies almost exactly on the celestial equator.
Arneb: Alpha Leporis, an F0 supergiant at 680 parsecs and one of the most luminous stars within its constellation. Its immense distance makes it one of the most intrinsically luminous stars with a traditional name.
Meissa: Lambda Orionis, a massive O-type star at 337 parsecs that ionizes a large ring-shaped emission nebula (Sh2-264) around it. It marks the head of Orion.
Hatysa: Iota Orionis, an O9 giant at 714 parsecs embedded in the Orion Nebula region. It is the brightest member of the asterism known as Orion's Sword and a massive spectroscopic binary.
Alnilam: Epsilon Orionis, the central and brightest star of Orion's Belt, a B0 supergiant at 606 parsecs. With a luminosity 275,000 times that of the Sun, it is one of the most luminous stars in the Milky Way visible to the naked eye.
Bubup: A G5 dwarf in Mensa at 38 parsecs with a Wolf-Rayet spectral designation in some catalogs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Australia. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Tianguan: Zeta Tauri, a B4 giant at 136 parsecs marking the southern horn-tip of Taurus. It is a shell star and the closest supernova remnant progenitor candidate, lying near the Crab Nebula (the remnant of SN 1054).
Phact: Alpha Columbae, a B7 subgiant at 80 parsecs and the brightest star in Columba the Dove. Its name means 'the ring dove' in Arabic, and it is a fast rotator.
Alnitak: Zeta Orionis, the easternmost star of Orion's Belt, a massive O9.5 supergiant at 226 parsecs. It illuminates the famous Horsehead and Flame nebulae, and is the brightest O-type star in the sky.
Saiph: Kappa Orionis, a B0.5 supergiant at 198 parsecs marking Orion's right foot. Despite appearing similar in brightness to Rigel, it emits most of its energy in the ultraviolet and is intrinsically very luminous.
Wazn: Beta Columbae, a K1 giant at 27 parsecs and the second brightest star in Columba. Its name means 'the weight' in Arabic.
Betelgeuse: Alpha Orionis, one of the largest known stars and a red M2 supergiant at 153 parsecs marking Orion's shoulder. Its dramatic dimming event in late 2019 captivated the world, and it is expected to explode as a supernova within the next 100,000 years.
Menkalinan: Beta Aurigae, an A2 eclipsing binary at 25 parsecs with two nearly identical components orbiting every 3.96 days. It is the second brightest star in Auriga.
Mahasim: Theta Aurigae, a chemically peculiar A0 star at 51 parsecs and a spectroscopic binary. It is a silicon star with an anomalously strong magnetic field.
Elkurud: Theta Columbae, a B8 subgiant at 221 parsecs in the constellation Columba. It is a relatively unremarkable blue star in a faint southern constellation.
Hoerikwaggo: Alpha Mensae, a G5 dwarf at 10 parsecs that closely resembles the Sun. Named after Table Mountain in South Africa, it is the brightest star in the constellation Mensa, though at magnitude 5.1 it is among the faintest alpha stars.
Amadioha: A G8/K0 subgiant in Canis Major at 56 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Nigeria after the Igbo god of thunder. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Propus: Eta Geminorum, an M3 giant at 118 parsecs that is a semi-regular variable and an eclipsing binary. Its name means 'the forward foot' of Gemini in Greek.
Red Rectangle: A bipolar proto-planetary nebula surrounding the B8 post-AGB star HD 44179 at 441 parsecs in Monoceros. Its distinctive rectangular shape and unusual infrared emission make it one of the most studied proto-planetary nebulae.
Furud: Zeta Canis Majoris, a B2.5 spectroscopic binary at 111 parsecs in Canis Major. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the solitary ones.'
Mirzam: Beta Canis Majoris, a B1 giant at 151 parsecs and the prototype of the Beta Cephei class of pulsating variables. It heralds the rising of Sirius, and its name means 'the herald' or 'the announcer' in Arabic.
Tejat: Mu Geminorum, an M3 irregular variable red giant at 71 parsecs marking the foot of Castor in Gemini. Its brightness varies between magnitudes 2.75 and 3.02 over a semi-regular period.
Canopus: Alpha Carinae, the second brightest star in the sky at magnitude -0.62, an F0 supergiant at 95 parsecs. It is a critical reference star for spacecraft navigation and was revered in ancient Egyptian and Polynesian cultures.
Lucilinburhuc: A G5 star in Auriga at 49 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Luxembourg. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Lusitania: A K5 star in Monoceros at 34 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Portugal after the historical region. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Ross 614: A faint M4.5 red dwarf flare star at only 4.1 parsecs in Monoceros. It is an astrometric binary with a period of 16.5 years and one of the nearest stars to the Sun.
Alhena: Gamma Geminorum, the third brightest star in Gemini, an A0 subgiant at 34 parsecs. Its name means 'the brand mark' in Arabic, and it marks the foot of Pollux.
Pipit: Nu Puppis, a B8 giant at 114 parsecs in the constellation Puppis. Named through the IAU initiative, it lies in a rich star-field region near the galactic plane.
Nosaxa: A G5 subgiant in Puppis at 85 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Mebsuta: Epsilon Geminorum, a yellow supergiant at 259 parsecs and one of the most luminous stars in Gemini. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the outstretched paw.'
Sirius: Alpha Canis Majoris, the brightest star in the night sky at magnitude -1.44, a white A0 dwarf just 2.6 parsecs away. Its white dwarf companion, Sirius B, was the first white dwarf to be discovered and confirmed, and the system has held cultural significance from ancient Egypt to the present.
Alzirr: Xi Geminorum, an F5 subgiant at 18 parsecs and one of the closer bright stars in Gemini. Its name derives from Arabic, meaning 'the button.'
Nervia: A G0 star in Auriga at 44 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Belgium. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Adhara: Epsilon Canis Majoris, the second brightest star in Canis Major and one of the brightest sources of extreme ultraviolet radiation in the sky. A B2 giant at 124 parsecs, it was the brightest star in the sky about 4.7 million years ago when it was closer to the Sun.
Citala: A G0 subgiant in Monoceros at 29 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by El Salvador. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Nganurganity: Sigma Canis Majoris, a luminous K4 supergiant at 344 parsecs, one of the most intrinsically luminous stars in the constellation. Its Aboriginal Australian name was given through the IAU initiative.
Muliphein: Gamma Canis Majoris, a B8 giant at 136 parsecs whose name likely derives from a corruption of an Arabic phrase. Despite its third-letter designation, it is notably fainter than many other stars in Canis Major.
Mekbuda: Zeta Geminorum, a classical Cepheid variable and G3 supergiant at 422 parsecs. It pulsates with a period of 10.15 days, making it one of the brighter Cepheids visible to the naked eye.
Wezen: Delta Canis Majoris, an F8 supergiant at 493 parsecs and one of the most luminous stars in the sky. Despite its unassuming appearance at magnitude 1.83, it radiates roughly 50,000 times the Sun's luminosity.
Wasat: Delta Geminorum, an F0 subgiant at 19 parsecs near the ecliptic, making it a frequent target for planetary occultations. Pluto was discovered near this star in 1930.
Aludra: Eta Canis Majoris, a B5 supergiant at 610 parsecs and one of the most luminous stars in the night sky. It is a candidate for eventually exploding as a supernova.
Gomeisa: Beta Canis Minoris, a B8 dwarf at 50 parsecs and the second brightest star in Canis Minor after Procyon. Its name derives from Arabic meaning 'the bleary-eyed one.'
Luyten's Star: A nearby M5 red dwarf at only 3.8 parsecs in Canis Minor, discovered by Willem Luyten. It is one of the closest stars to the Sun and is known to host at least two confirmed exoplanets.
Castor: Alpha Geminorum, one of the finest multiple star systems in the sky, consisting of six stars in three binary pairs at 16 parsecs. The two brightest components, both hot A-type stars, are resolvable in small telescopes.
Jishui: Omicron Geminorum, an F3 giant at 51 parsecs in Gemini. Its Chinese-derived name references a historical asterism, and it lies near the ecliptic.
Procyon: Alpha Canis Minoris, the eighth brightest star in the sky at magnitude 0.4, an F5 subgiant at only 3.5 parsecs. Its white dwarf companion, Procyon B, was predicted from astrometric wobble before it was directly observed.
Ceibo: A K4 dwarf in Chamaeleon at 35 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Uruguay after the national flower. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Pollux: Beta Geminorum, the brightest star in Gemini and the closest giant star to the Sun at 10 parsecs. It hosts a confirmed Jupiter-mass exoplanet, making it the nearest star with an exoplanet visible to the naked eye.
Tapecue: A G6 dwarf in Carina at 33 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Paraguay. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Azmidi: Xi Puppis, a luminous G6 supergiant at 368 parsecs in the constellation Puppis. It is one of the most luminous G-type stars visible to the naked eye.
Naos: Zeta Puppis, one of the hottest and most luminous stars visible to the naked eye, an O5 supergiant at 332 parsecs. With a surface temperature exceeding 40,000 K and a powerful stellar wind, it is a textbook example of a massive hot star.
Tureis: Rho Puppis, the brightest Delta Scuti variable star, an F2 giant at 19 parsecs. It pulsates with a primary period of about 3.4 hours.
Tegmine: Zeta Cancri, a remarkable multiple star system at 25 parsecs consisting of at least four gravitationally bound stars. It is one of the finest multiple-star telescopic targets in the sky.
Tarf: Beta Cancri, the brightest star in the faint constellation Cancer, a K4 giant at 93 parsecs. It hosts a candidate exoplanet, and its name derives from the Arabic for 'the eye.'
Nasti: A G0 star in Ursa Major at 55 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Piautos: Lambda Cancri, a B9.5 dwarf at 140 parsecs in Cancer. It is a faint and relatively unremarkable star whose name derives from a historical asterism.
Avior: Epsilon Carinae, a binary system at 186 parsecs consisting of a K3 giant and a hot B2 companion. Its name was assigned by the Royal Air Force during World War II for navigational use, as many bright southern stars lacked common names.
Alsciaukat: 31 Lyncis, a K5 giant at 117 parsecs and the brightest named star in the constellation Lynx. Its name is derived from the Arabic for 'the thorn.'
Muscida: Omicron Ursae Majoris, a G4 giant at 55 parsecs marking the nose of the Great Bear. It is a binary system with a faint red dwarf companion.
Minchir: Sigma Hydrae, a K2 giant at 114 parsecs in the long constellation Hydra. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the nose of the snake.'
Gakyid: A G5 star in Cancer at 81 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Bhutan. The name means 'happiness' in Dzongkha, and it hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Meleph: Epsilon Cancri, an Am metallic-line star at 180 parsecs in the Beehive Cluster (M44). It is one of the brighter individual members of this famous open cluster.
Asellus Borealis: Gamma Cancri, an A1 subgiant at 56 parsecs whose name means 'the northern donkey.' Together with Asellus Australis, it flanks the Beehive Cluster, representing two donkeys feeding at a manger.
Asellus Australis: Delta Cancri, a K0 giant at 40 parsecs whose name means 'the southern donkey.' It lies on the ecliptic and is frequently occulted by the Moon, making it useful for astrometric measurements.
Alsephina: Delta Velorum, an A1 dwarf at 25 parsecs and the brightest eclipsing binary in the sky visible to the naked eye. It is part of the False Cross asterism in the southern sky.
Ashlesha: Epsilon Hydrae, a G0 subgiant at 40 parsecs and a multiple star system. Its name comes from a Hindu nakshatra (lunar mansion) associated with a serpent.
Copernicus: 55 Cancri (Rho-1 Cancri), a G8 dwarf at 12 parsecs hosting one of the most well-characterized exoplanetary systems with at least five known planets. Named after the astronomer Nicolaus Copernicus, it is a benchmark system for exoplanet studies.
Stribor: A G0 star in Lynx at 76 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Croatia after a deity from Slavic mythology. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Acubens: Alpha Cancri, an A5 metallic-line star at 58 parsecs and the brightest star in Cancer, though at magnitude 4.26 it is rather faint. Its name means 'the claw' in Arabic.
Talitha: Iota Ursae Majoris, an A7 subgiant at 15 parsecs and a multiple star system in Ursa Major. Its name means 'the third leap' in Arabic, referencing one of three pairs of stars imagined as gazelle tracks.
Ross 687: A faint M4 red dwarf at 21 parsecs in Hydra. It is one of the many nearby red dwarfs cataloged by Frank Ross in the early 20th century.
Alkaphrah: Kappa Ursae Majoris, an A1 binary star at 110 parsecs on the ear of the Great Bear. Its two nearly identical A-type components orbit each other every 36 years.
Suhail: Lambda Velorum, a K4 supergiant at 167 parsecs and one of the brightest stars in Vela. Its name has been applied to several bright southern stars in Arabic tradition and it is a semi-regular pulsating variable.
Nahn: Xi Cancri, a K0 giant at 114 parsecs in Cancer. It is one of several faint named stars in this dim zodiacal constellation.
Miaplacidus: Beta Carinae, the second brightest star in Carina at magnitude 1.67, an A2 subgiant at 35 parsecs. Its name may combine Arabic and Latin roots meaning 'placid waters.'
Aspidiske: Iota Carinae, a luminous A8 supergiant at 235 parsecs in Carina. It was formerly part of the great constellation Argo Navis and is one of the brightest stars in the southern sky.
Markeb: Kappa Velorum, a B2 subgiant at 175 parsecs that is part of the False Cross asterism sometimes confused with the Southern Cross. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'something to ride.'
Alphard: Alpha Hydrae, the solitary bright star of the long constellation Hydra, a K3 giant at 55 parsecs. Known as 'the solitary one,' it stands alone in a dim region of sky, and its distinctly orange hue is visible to the naked eye.
Intercrus: A K0 giant in Ursa Major at 86 parsecs, notable for hosting a confirmed exoplanet. Its Latin name means 'between the legs,' referring to its position in the constellation.
Alterf: Lambda Leonis, a K5 giant at 101 parsecs marking the mouth of the Lion. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the glance (of the lion).'
Illyrian: A G0 star in Leo Minor at 125 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Albania. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Kalausi: A K0 dwarf in Vela at 41 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Kenya. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Ukdah: Iota Hydrae, a K3 variable giant at 81 parsecs in Hydra. Its name means 'the knot' in Arabic, marking a significant point along the serpent's body.
Subra: Omicron Leonis, an A5 dwarf with a faint companion at 40 parsecs. Its name derives from Arabic and it marks the front paw of the Lion.
Ananuca: A faint M-type red dwarf in Vela at just 10 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Chile after a native wildflower. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Ras Elased Australis: Epsilon Leonis, a G0 bright giant at 76 parsecs and part of the 'head of the Lion' asterism. Its verbose name means 'the southern head of the lion' in a blend of Arabic and Latin.
Natasha: A K1 dwarf in Vela at 32 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Mozambique. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Zhang: Upsilon-1 Hydrae, a G6/G8 giant at 81 parsecs in Hydra. Its Chinese-derived name references a historical asterism meaning 'extended net.'
Rasalas: Mu Leonis, a K0 giant at 38 parsecs forming part of the 'head of the Lion' with Epsilon Leonis. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the head of the lion.'
Felis: A K5 giant in Hydra at 188 parsecs, historically placed in the now-defunct constellation Felis (the Cat) created by Lalande. It is one of the few stars that preserve the memory of an obsolete constellation.
Bibha: An F8 star in Sextans at 95 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by India after the physicist Bibha Chowdhuri. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Yunu: A K4 giant in Leo at 91 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. Its name derives from East Asian mythology.
Regulus: Alpha Leonis, the brightest star in Leo and the 21st brightest in the sky, a B7 dwarf at 24 parsecs. It is one of the fastest-spinning bright stars known, rotating at 96% of its breakup velocity and rendering it distinctly oblate.
Adhafera: Zeta Leonis, an F0 giant at 84 parsecs forming part of the 'sickle' asterism in Leo. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the curl' or 'the lock of hair.'
Tania Borealis: Lambda Ursae Majoris, an A2 subgiant at 42 parsecs that forms a naked-eye pair with Tania Australis. Together they represent 'the second leap' of the gazelle in Arabic star lore.
Algieba: Gamma Leonis, a magnificent double star at 40 parsecs consisting of a K0 giant and G7 giant resolvable in small telescopes. The pair's golden and orange hues make it one of the finest visual doubles in the sky.
Tania Australis: Mu Ursae Majoris, an M0 giant at 71 parsecs that forms a pair with Tania Borealis. It is a semi-regular variable with a slow brightness fluctuation.
Shaomin: Rho Leonis, a B1 supergiant at an extreme distance, the most luminous star in Leo. Its spectral classification suggests it may be a runaway blue supergiant.
Macondo: A K3 dwarf in Antlia at 28 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Colombia after the fictional town in Gabriel Garcia Marquez's works. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Praecipua: 46 Leonis Minoris, a K0 subgiant at 29 parsecs and the brightest star in Leo Minor. Despite being the constellation's brightest, it was not assigned a Greek letter designation due to a historical mapping error.
Aiolos: An A8 giant in Carina at 90 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Greece. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet and a directly imaged planetary-mass companion.
Chalawan: 47 Ursae Majoris, a G0 dwarf at 14 parsecs hosting at least three known exoplanets. Named after a mythical Thai crocodile, it is one of the most Sun-like planet-hosting stars.
Alkes: Alpha Crateris, the brightest star in Crater the Cup, a K1 giant at 49 parsecs. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the cup.'
Merak: Beta Ursae Majoris, an A1 dwarf at 24 parsecs and one of the two 'Pointer Stars' in the Big Dipper that point to Polaris. It has a significant infrared excess suggesting a dusty debris disk.
Lalande 21185: One of the closest stars to the Sun at just 2.5 parsecs, an M2 red dwarf in Ursa Major. Despite being invisible to the naked eye, it is the brightest red dwarf in the northern sky and a frequent target for exoplanet searches.
Dubhe: Alpha Ursae Majoris, the second brightest star in the Big Dipper and the upper of the two Pointer Stars that indicate Polaris. An orange K0 giant spectroscopic binary at 38 parsecs, it does not share the common proper motion of most Big Dipper stars.
Dingolay: A K0 star in Leo at 158 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Trinidad and Tobago. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Zosma: Delta Leonis, an A4 dwarf at 18 parsecs on the back of the Lion. Its name means 'the girdle' in Greek, and it is a relatively young star that will evolve into a giant within 600 million years.
Chertan: Theta Leonis, an A2 dwarf at 51 parsecs on the hip of Leo. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'two small ribs.'
Hunahpu: A K0 subgiant in Crater at 134 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Honduras after a hero in the Mayan Popol Vuh. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Alula Borealis: Nu Ursae Majoris, a K3 giant at 122 parsecs that forms a naked-eye pair with Alula Australis. The name means 'the first leap (northern),' referencing gazelle footprints.
Shama: A K0 star in Leo at 50 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Pakistan. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Giausar: Lambda Draconis, an M0 giant at 102 parsecs in the tail of the Dragon. It is a red giant with a distinctly orange-red hue.
Formosa: A G9 giant in Leo at 122 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Sagarmatha: A K0 star in Leo at 50 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Nepal after Mount Sagarmatha (Everest). It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Noquisi: A faint M3 red dwarf at 10 parsecs in Leo, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. It is one of the closer planet-hosting stars.
Uklun: A G6 dwarf in Centaurus at 40 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Flegetonte: A K0 star in Virgo at 30 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Italy after the river of fire in Greek mythology. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Taiyangshou: Chi Ursae Majoris, a K0 giant at 56 parsecs in the body of the Great Bear. Its Chinese name means 'the guardian of the Sun.'
Ross 128: A nearby M4.5 red dwarf at 3.4 parsecs in Virgo, one of the closest stars to the Sun. It hosts a confirmed Earth-mass exoplanet in its habitable zone, making it one of the most promising targets for habitability studies.
Denebola: Beta Leonis, the second brightest star in Leo at magnitude 2.14, an A3 dwarf at 11 parsecs. Its name means 'the tail of the lion,' and it is a Delta Scuti variable with a debris disk suggesting possible planetary formation.
Zavijava: Beta Virginis, an F8 dwarf at 11 parsecs and one of the closest bright stars in Virgo. It was used by Einstein's collaborators to test general relativity during the 1922 solar eclipse.
Aniara: A K0 giant in Ursa Major at 126 parsecs, named after the Swedish epic poem about a spacecraft. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Groombridge 1830: A G8 subdwarf at 9 parsecs in Ursa Major with the third-highest proper motion of any star, a galactic halo visitor passing through the solar neighborhood. It moves across the sky at about 7 arcseconds per year.
Phecda: Gamma Ursae Majoris, an A0 dwarf at 26 parsecs forming the lower-left corner of the Big Dipper's bowl. It is a member of the Ursa Major moving group sharing a common origin with most other Dipper stars.
Tonatiuh: A G9 giant in Camelopardalis at 97 parsecs, named after the Aztec sun god through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Mexico. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Alchiba: Alpha Corvi, an F0 dwarf at 15 parsecs and the faintest Alpha star among the classical constellations. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the tent.'
Imai: Delta Crucis, a B2 subgiant at 106 parsecs and a Beta Cephei variable forming the base of the Southern Cross. It is the faintest of the four main Cross stars.
Megrez: Delta Ursae Majoris, an A3 dwarf at 25 parsecs that connects the handle and bowl of the Big Dipper. It is the faintest of the seven major Dipper stars, and its name means 'the root of the tail.'
Gienah: Gamma Corvi, the brightest star in Corvus the Crow, a B8 giant at 47 parsecs. Its name means 'the wing' in Arabic.
Zaniah: Eta Virginis, an A2 subgiant at 81 parsecs in Virgo. It is a triple star system where the two closest components orbit each other in less than a day.
Ginan: Epsilon Crucis, a K3/K4 giant at 70 parsecs forming the fifth, smaller star sometimes added to the Southern Cross. Its name comes from an Aboriginal Australian story about a bag of songs.
Tupa: An F8/G0 dwarf in Crux at 38 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Brazil after the Tupi-Guarani supreme god. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Acrux: Alpha Crucis, the brightest star in Crux and the 13th brightest in the sky, a B0.5 subgiant at 99 parsecs. It is a multiple system whose combined light marks the foot of the Southern Cross, and it appears on the flags of Australia, New Zealand, Brazil, and other southern nations.
3C 273: The brightest quasar as seen from Earth, a luminous active galactic nucleus in Virgo at a cosmological distance. Discovered in 1963 by Maarten Schmidt, it was the first quasar to have its redshift measured, revolutionizing our understanding of the distant universe.
Algorab: Delta Corvi, a B9.5 dwarf at 27 parsecs in Corvus the Crow. Its name means 'the crow' in Arabic, and it is a wide double star with an unrelated K-type background star.
Gacrux: Gamma Crucis, the nearest M-type giant to the Sun at 27 parsecs and the top star of the Southern Cross. Its distinctly red color contrasts beautifully with the blue-white of the other Cross stars.
Funi: A G0 star in Draco at 66 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Chara: Beta Canum Venaticorum, a G0 dwarf at 8.4 parsecs that is one of the most Sun-like nearby stars. Its name means 'joy' in Greek and it has been a frequent target in searches for Earth-like exoplanets.
Kraz: Beta Corvi, a G5 bright giant at 45 parsecs and the brightest star in Corvus despite its Beta designation. It is likely nearing the end of its giant-branch evolution.
Phyllon Kissinou: 23 Comae Berenices, an A0 subgiant at 95 parsecs in the constellation Coma Berenices. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Porrima: Gamma Virginis, a beautiful double star at 12 parsecs consisting of two nearly identical F0 dwarfs. With a 169-year orbit, the pair has been closing and separating for telescopic observers since the 18th century.
La Superba: Y Canum Venaticorum, one of the reddest stars visible in the sky, a carbon star in Canes Venatici at 321 parsecs. Its intense ruby color, caused by carbon molecules absorbing blue light, inspired astronomer Angelo Secchi to call it 'La Superba.'
Tianyi: 7 Draconis, a K5 giant at 227 parsecs whose Chinese name means 'the celestial one.' It lies in the circumpolar constellation Draco.
Mimosa: Beta Crucis, the second brightest star in Crux and a massive B0.5 giant at 85 parsecs. It is a Beta Cephei variable that pulsates in multiple modes, and its blue-white light forms a key part of the Southern Cross.
Gar: A faint M4 red dwarf at 8.4 parsecs in Virgo, one of the closest known planet-hosting stars. Named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign.
Alioth: Epsilon Ursae Majoris, the brightest star in Ursa Major and the Big Dipper at magnitude 1.76, an A0 chemically peculiar star at 25 parsecs. It is the brightest of the Alpha-2 Canum Venaticorum type of magnetic variable stars.
Taiyi: 8 Draconis, an A5 star at 29 parsecs in Draco and a Delta Scuti variable. Its Chinese name means 'the supreme one.'
Minelauva: Delta Virginis, an M3 red giant at 61 parsecs in Virgo. It is a semi-regular variable star with a rich red hue visible in binoculars.
Cor Caroli B: Alpha-1 Canum Venaticorum, the fainter F0 component of the Cor Caroli double star system at 37 parsecs. It is the less-studied companion to the famous magnetic variable.
Cor Caroli: Alpha-2 Canum Venaticorum, the prototype of a class of magnetic chemically peculiar variable stars at 35 parsecs. Named 'the heart of Charles' (King Charles II), it displays spectral variations as its strong magnetic field rotates different chemical patches into view.
Vindemiatrix: Epsilon Virginis, a G8 giant at 34 parsecs whose name means 'the grape gatherer.' In antiquity, its heliacal rising coincided with the grape harvest season.
Diadem: Alpha Comae Berenices, an F5 binary at 18 parsecs and the brightest star in Coma Berenices. The nearly equal components orbit almost edge-on, making it a near-eclipsing system.
Kulou: Iota Centauri, an A2 dwarf at 18 parsecs in Centaurus. It is one of the closer bright A-type stars in the southern sky.
Mizar: Zeta Ursae Majoris, the famous double star at the bend of the Big Dipper's handle at 26 parsecs. Along with its naked-eye companion Alcor, it forms one of the most celebrated visual pairs in the sky, and it was the first telescopic double star discovered (by Riccioli in 1650).
Matza: An A2 star in Centaurus at 98 parsecs. It was named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign and hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Spica: Alpha Virginis, the brightest star in Virgo and the 16th brightest in the sky, a B1 dwarf at 77 parsecs. It is a close spectroscopic binary with tidally distorted egg-shaped components, and its heliacal setting may have influenced the orientation of the temple at Thebes.
Alcor: 80 Ursae Majoris, the fainter companion to Mizar in the Big Dipper at 25 parsecs. The ability to see both Mizar and Alcor was used in ancient times as a test of visual acuity, and the pair are now known to be a true physical binary.
Dofida: A G2 dwarf in Centaurus at 38 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Ghana. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Liesma: A K0 star in Ursa Major at 89 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Latvia. The name means 'flame' in Latvian, and it hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Heze: Zeta Virginis, an A3 dwarf at 23 parsecs in Virgo. It is a relatively unremarkable main-sequence star notable mainly for its position in the constellation figure.
Alkaid: Eta Ursae Majoris, the tip of the Big Dipper's handle and the third brightest star in Ursa Major, a B3 dwarf at 32 parsecs. Unlike most Dipper stars, it does not belong to the Ursa Major moving group.
Heng: Nu Centauri, a B2 subgiant at 134 parsecs in Centaurus. Its Chinese-derived name references a historical asterism.
Muphrid: Eta Bootis, a G0 subgiant at 11 parsecs and the second brightest star in Bootes after Arcturus. It lies only 3.3 light-years from Arcturus -- from Arcturus, Muphrid would appear as bright as Venus does from Earth.
Leepwal: Zeta Centauri, a B2.5 subgiant at 117 parsecs in Centaurus. Named through the IAU initiative, it is a hot, luminous star in the Centaurus OB association.
Hadar: Beta Centauri, the 11th brightest star in the sky and a B1 giant at 120 parsecs. Together with Rigil Kentaurus, it forms the 'Southern Pointers' that indicate the direction to the Southern Cross.
Thuban: Alpha Draconis, an A0 giant at 93 parsecs that served as the North Star around 2700 BCE when the Pyramids of Giza were built. Precession has since moved the celestial pole to Polaris.
Menkent: Theta Centauri, a K0 giant at 18 parsecs and the third brightest star in Centaurus. It is one of the closest K-type giants to the Sun.
Kang: Kappa Virginis, a K3 giant at 78 parsecs in Virgo. Its Chinese name references a historical asterism in the Azure Dragon mansion system.
Arcturus: Alpha Bootis, the brightest star in the northern celestial hemisphere and the fourth brightest overall at magnitude -0.05, a K2 giant at 11 parsecs. A red giant approaching from the galactic halo, it moves through the local stellar population at high velocity and was the first star whose light was used to open a world's fair (Chicago, 1933).
Syrma: Iota Virginis, an F7 dwarf at 22 parsecs in Virgo. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the train of the garment.'
Xuange: Lambda Bootis, the prototype of the Lambda Bootis class of chemically peculiar stars at 30 parsecs. These metal-poor A-type stars may have accreted gas from circumstellar material while metals were removed by radiation pressure.
Khambalia: Lambda Virginis, an A1 dwarf at 57 parsecs in Virgo. Its name is of Coptic origin, meaning 'crooked-clawed.'
Elgafar: Phi Virginis, a G2 giant at 36 parsecs in Virgo. Its Arabic name references one of the stars covering or hiding something.
Proxima Centauri: The closest known star to the Sun at just 1.3 parsecs, a faint M5 red dwarf flare star in Centaurus. It hosts at least two confirmed exoplanets, including Proxima b in the habitable zone, and it is gravitationally bound to the Alpha Centauri system.
Seginus: Gamma Bootis, an A7 giant at 27 parsecs in Bootes. It is a Delta Scuti variable with small, rapid brightness oscillations.
Toliman: Alpha Centauri B, a K1 dwarf at 1.3 parsecs and the fainter of the two main components of the closest star system to the Sun. It is slightly smaller and cooler than the Sun.
Rigil Kentaurus: Alpha Centauri A, the brighter component of the nearest star system to the Sun at 1.3 parsecs, a G2 dwarf very similar to the Sun. Together with Toliman and Proxima Centauri, it forms a hierarchical triple system.
Uridim: Alpha Lupi, a B1.5 giant at 142 parsecs and the brightest star in Lupus. It is a Beta Cephei pulsating variable.
Xami: Alpha Circini, an F1 star at 17 parsecs and the brightest rapidly oscillating Ap star known. It pulsates with a 6.8-minute period driven by its strong magnetic field.
Izar: Epsilon Bootis, a spectacular double star at 62 parsecs consisting of a K0 giant and an A2 dwarf. Known as 'Pulcherrima' (most beautiful) for its striking orange-and-blue color contrast in telescopes.
Monch: A K0 star in Virgo at 32 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Switzerland after the famous Bernese Alps peak. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Paradys: Alpha Apodis, a K5 giant at 137 parsecs and the brightest star in the Bird of Paradise constellation. It lies in the deep southern sky near the south celestial pole.
Merga: 38 Bootis, an F7 subgiant at 49 parsecs in Bootes. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the chained woman,' possibly a corruption of an older star name.
Solitaire: 58 Hydrae, a K3 giant at 102 parsecs in the southern portion of Hydra. Its Latin-derived name reflects its isolated position in the sky.
Kochab: Beta Ursae Minoris, a K4 giant at 40 parsecs that served as the closest 'pole star' to the north celestial pole around 1500 BCE. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet and is the brightest star in the Little Dipper's bowl.
Zubenelgenubi: Alpha-2 Librae, an A3 subgiant at 23 parsecs and the traditional 'southern claw' of the Scorpion, predating its assignment to Libra. It is a wide visual double star resolvable to the naked eye.
Arcalis: A K0 star in Bootes at 110 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Andorra. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Baekdu: A K0 star in Ursa Minor at 160 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by the Republic of Korea after the sacred mountain. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Nekkar: Beta Bootis, a G8 giant at 69 parsecs and one of the stars marking the head of Bootes. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the herdsman.'
Quadrans: 44 Bootis, a G2 dwarf contact binary at 13 parsecs that lies near the location of the defunct constellation Quadrans Muralis. This obsolete constellation gives its name to the annual Quadrantid meteor shower.
Brachium: Sigma Librae, an M3/M4 red giant at 88 parsecs whose name means 'the arm.' It was historically known as Gamma Scorpii before being reassigned to Libra.
Zubeneschamali: Beta Librae, the brightest star in Libra and a B8 dwarf at 57 parsecs. Its name means 'the northern claw,' and it has been described by some observers as having a faintly greenish tint, making it one of very few stars with this distinction.
Nikawiy: A G5 star in Bootes at 98 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Canada. The Cree word means 'mother,' and the star hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Pherkad: Gamma Ursae Minoris, an A3 giant at 149 parsecs that together with Kochab forms the 'Guards of the Pole' in the Little Dipper. It is a Delta Scuti variable with a slight brightness fluctuation.
Alkalurops: Mu-1 Bootis, an F0 dwarf at 35 parsecs in Bootes, part of an attractive triple star system. Its name means 'the herdsman's staff' in Arabic-Greek hybrid.
Alkalurops B: Mu-2 Bootis, the G1 dwarf companion to Mu-1 Bootis at 36 parsecs. It is itself a close double, making the Alkalurops system a hierarchical triple.
Edasich: Iota Draconis, a K2 giant at 31 parsecs and one of the first giant stars found to host an exoplanet (discovered in 2002). Its planet has a highly eccentric orbit.
Nusakan: Beta Coronae Borealis, an F0 peculiar star at 34 parsecs in the Northern Crown. It is a spectroscopic binary with the prototype of the rapidly oscillating Ap star class.
Alphecca: Alpha Coronae Borealis, the brightest star in the Northern Crown, an A0 dwarf at 23 parsecs. It is an eclipsing binary with properties similar to Algol, though with much shallower eclipses.
Zubenelhakrabi: Gamma Librae, a K0 giant at 50 parsecs that was historically the 'claws of the scorpion.' It is the third brightest star in Libra.
Karaka: A K0/K1 dwarf in Apus at 39 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by New Zealand after the native karaka tree. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Unukalhai: Alpha Serpentis, a K2 giant at 23 parsecs and the brightest star in Serpens. Its Arabic name means 'the neck of the snake,' marking the serpent's throat.
Zhou: Beta Serpentis, an A3 dwarf at 48 parsecs in the head of Serpens Caput. Its Chinese-derived name references a historical asterism.
Gudja: Kappa Serpentis, an M1 giant at 117 parsecs with a distinctly reddish hue. It lies in the head region of Serpens Caput.
Iklil: Rho Scorpii, a B2 subgiant at 145 parsecs near the head of Scorpius. Its Arabic name means 'the crown' or 'the wreath.'
Fang: Pi Scorpii, a massive B1+B2 binary at 180 parsecs near the head of the Scorpion. It is one of several hot, luminous stars marking the claws and forehead of Scorpius.
Dschubba: Delta Scorpii, a B0.2 subgiant at 151 parsecs that unexpectedly began brightening in 2000, transitioning from a normal B star to a Be emission-line star. This dramatic outburst doubled its brightness and has continued intermittently.
Acrab: Beta-1 Scorpii, a B0.5 dwarf at 124 parsecs and part of a sextuple star system at the head of Scorpius. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the scorpion.'
Acrab B: Beta-2 Scorpii, the B2 companion to Acrab at 122 parsecs. The pair is resolvable in small telescopes and each component is itself a spectroscopic binary.
Marsic: Kappa Herculis, a G8 giant at 113 parsecs in Hercules. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the elbow.'
Marsic B: The K1 giant companion to Kappa Herculis at a much greater distance (645 parsecs), suggesting it may be an optical rather than physical companion.
Kamuy: A K0 giant in Corona Borealis at 125 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Japan after the Ainu word for a divine being. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Jabbah: Nu Scorpii, a complex multiple star system at 145 parsecs near the head of Scorpius. At least seven components make it one of the richest known stellar systems, embedded in the Rho Ophiuchi cloud complex.
Sharjah: A faint M-type red dwarf at 14 parsecs in Scorpius, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by the United Arab Emirates. It is one of the closer planet-hosting stars.
Yed Prior: Delta Ophiuchi, an M1 giant at 52 parsecs forming a naked-eye pair with Yed Posterior. Together they represent 'the hand' of Ophiuchus holding the serpent.
Yed Posterior: Epsilon Ophiuchi, a G8 giant at 33 parsecs forming the other half of 'the hand' pair with Yed Prior. Its name comes from the Arabic 'yad' meaning 'hand.'
Hunor: An F8 star in Hercules at 114 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Hungary. It hosts a confirmed 'hot Jupiter' exoplanet.
Alniyat: Sigma Scorpii, a B1 giant at 214 parsecs near the heart of Scorpius. Its name means 'the arteries' in Arabic, and it is a complex quadruple system embedded in bright nebulosity.
Athebyne: Eta Draconis, a G8 giant at 28 parsecs near the head of the Dragon. It is one of the closer giant stars to the Sun and a helpful guide to locating Draco.
Cujam: Omega Herculis, a chemically peculiar B9 star at 77 parsecs in Hercules. Its name may derive from Latin.
Timir: A K0 subgiant in Ophiuchus at 59 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Bangladesh. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Antares: Alpha Scorpii, the 'heart of the Scorpion' and the 15th brightest star in the sky, a red M1 supergiant with a B2.5 companion at 170 parsecs. One of the largest stars visible to the naked eye, it would engulf the orbit of Mars if placed at the Sun's position.
Kornephoros: Beta Herculis, a G8 giant at 43 parsecs and the brightest star in Hercules. Its name derives from the Greek for 'the club-bearer.'
Wolf 1061: A nearby M4 red dwarf at 4.3 parsecs in Ophiuchus, hosting at least three confirmed exoplanets. One of its planets, Wolf 1061c, lies near the inner edge of the habitable zone.
Ogma: A G0 star in Hercules at 79 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Ireland after the Celtic god of eloquence. It hosts a confirmed 'hot Saturn' exoplanet.
Marfik: Lambda Ophiuchi, an A2 dwarf at 53 parsecs in Ophiuchus. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the elbow.'
Rosalidecastro: A G0 star in Ophiuchus at 62 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Spain after the Galician poet Rosalia de Castro. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Paikauhale: Tau Scorpii, a hot B0 dwarf at 145 parsecs in Scorpius with an unusually strong and complex magnetic field. It is a slow rotator for its spectral type, possibly due to magnetic braking.
Atria: Alpha Trianguli Australis, the brightest star in the Southern Triangle, a K2 giant at 120 parsecs. It is a spectroscopic binary and an important navigation star in the southern hemisphere.
Larawag: Epsilon Scorpii, a K2 giant at 20 parsecs near the tail of Scorpius. Its Aboriginal Australian name was given through the IAU initiative, and it is the closest star in the main body of Scorpius.
Xamidimura: Mu-1 Scorpii, a B1.5 eclipsing binary at 154 parsecs in the tail of Scorpius. Its Khoisan name was given through the IAU initiative, and both components are massive blue stars.
Pipirima: Mu-2 Scorpii, a B2 subgiant at 145 parsecs forming a wide pair with Mu-1 Scorpii (Xamidimura). The pair are not physically related but create an attractive naked-eye double.
Mahsati: A K0 star in Ophiuchus at 186 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Azerbaijan after the medieval poet. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Rapeto: A G2 subgiant in Scorpius at 50 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Madagascar. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Aldhibah: Zeta Draconis, a B6 giant at 101 parsecs in the body of the Dragon. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the male hyena.'
Sabik: Eta Ophiuchi, the second brightest star in Ophiuchus, an A2.5 dwarf at 27 parsecs. It is a close binary whose components orbit every 88 years and are difficult to resolve.
Rasalgethi: Alpha Herculis, a semi-regular variable M5 supergiant at 110 parsecs and one of the largest stars visible to the naked eye. It forms a beautiful color-contrast double with its blue-green companion.
Sarin: Delta Herculis, an A3 subgiant at 23 parsecs in the Keystone asterism of Hercules. It is a spectroscopic binary with a period of about 10 years.
Guniibuu: 36 Ophiuchi A, a K2 dwarf at just 5.9 parsecs in Ophiuchus, part of a triple system of K-type dwarfs. Its Aboriginal Australian name was given through the IAU initiative.
Inquill: A G1 dwarf in Ara at 55 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Peru. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Rastaban: Beta Draconis, a G2 bright giant at 117 parsecs in the head of the Dragon. Its name means 'the head of the serpent' in Arabic.
Maasym: Lambda Herculis, a K3 variable giant at 113 parsecs in Hercules. Its name derives from Arabic and marks the hero's forearm.
Lesath: Upsilon Scorpii, a B2 subgiant at 177 parsecs near the stinger of Scorpius. Together with Shaula, it forms a close pair at the tip of the Scorpion's tail, sometimes called the 'Cat's Eyes.'
Yildun: Delta Ursae Minoris, an A1 dwarf at 53 parsecs in the Little Dipper. Its Turkish-derived name means 'star,' and it is the faintest of the named stars in Ursa Minor.
Shaula: Lambda Scorpii, the second brightest star in Scorpius at magnitude 1.62, a B1.5 subgiant at 175 parsecs. It marks the tip of the Scorpion's stinger and is a triple star system.
Rasalhague: Alpha Ophiuchi, the brightest star in Ophiuchus, an A5 giant at 15 parsecs. Its name means 'the head of the serpent-charmer,' and it is a fast rotator significantly oblate in shape.
Sargas: Theta Scorpii, an F1 bright giant at 92 parsecs in the tail of Scorpius. It is the brightest F-type star in Scorpius and one of the most southerly named stars visible from mid-northern latitudes.
Dziban: Psi-1 Draconis, an F5 subgiant at 23 parsecs that forms a wide double with Psi-2 Draconis. Both components host confirmed exoplanets, making them a rare planet-hosting visual binary pair.
Dziban B: Psi-2 Draconis, a G0 dwarf at 23 parsecs forming a wide pair with Psi-1 Draconis. Like its companion, it hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Cebalrai: Beta Ophiuchi, a K2 giant at 25 parsecs and the second brightest star in Ophiuchus. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the shepherd's dog.'
Alruba: An A2 dwarf at 163 parsecs in Draco. Historically uncataloged by Bayer or Flamsteed, it received its traditional name from Arabic star lore.
Cervantes: Mu Arae, a G5 dwarf at 16 parsecs in Ara hosting at least four known exoplanets. Named after the Spanish author Miguel de Cervantes, its planetary system includes one of the first detected 'hot Neptunes.'
Bake-eo: Gamma Ophiuchi, an A0 dwarf at 32 parsecs in Ophiuchus. Named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign, it marks the head of the Serpent-Bearer.
Fuyue: A K0/K1 giant at 39 parsecs in Scorpius. Its Chinese-derived name references the historical asterism 'the Ax,' located between Scorpius and Ophiuchus.
Grumium: Xi Draconis, a K2 giant at 35 parsecs in the jaw of the Dragon. Its Latin name means 'the jaw' or 'the snout.'
Eltanin: Gamma Draconis, the brightest star in Draco, a K5 giant at 47 parsecs. It is historically important because James Bradley's observations of its aberration of starlight in 1728 provided the first direct evidence that Earth orbits the Sun.
Barnard's Star: The star with the largest known proper motion (10.4 arcseconds/year), an M4 subdwarf at just 1.8 parsecs in Ophiuchus. The second closest stellar system to the Sun, it is a very old, low-metallicity red dwarf moving rapidly through the solar neighborhood.
Bodu: 95 Herculis, a G2 giant at 128 parsecs notable as a visual binary with a striking color contrast. Named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by the Maldives.
Pincoya: A K2 star in Sagittarius at 41 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Chile. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Alnasl: Gamma-2 Sagittarii, a K0 giant at 30 parsecs marking the tip of the arrow in Sagittarius. Its name means 'the arrowhead' in Arabic.
Ramus: 102 Herculis, a B2 subgiant at 281 parsecs in Hercules. It is a hot, luminous blue star in the northern sky.
Polis: Mu Sagittarii, a B2 giant at extreme distance in the top of the Sagittarius 'Teapot' asterism. It is a complex multiple system whose components span a wide range of spectral types.
Kaus Media: Delta Sagittarii, a K3 giant at 107 parsecs forming the middle of the Archer's bow. Its name means 'the middle of the bow' in Arabic-Latin hybrid.
Alasia: A G5 dwarf in Serpens at 43 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Cyprus. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Tusizuo: 109 Herculis, a K2 giant at 36 parsecs in Hercules. Its Chinese-derived name references a historical asterism.
Kaus Australis: Epsilon Sagittarii, the brightest star in Sagittarius at magnitude 1.79, a B9.5 giant at 44 parsecs. It marks the base of the Archer's bow and lies near the direction of the Milky Way's center.
Fafnir: 42 Draconis, a K2 giant at 97 parsecs in the circumpolar constellation Draco. Named after the dragon from Norse mythology, it hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Kaus Borealis: Lambda Sagittarii, a K1 giant at 24 parsecs forming the top of the Archer's bow. Its name means 'the top of the bow,' and it is the closest of the main Sagittarius stars.
Vega: Alpha Lyrae, the fifth brightest star in the sky and one of the most extensively studied stars in astrophysics, an A0 dwarf at 7.7 parsecs. It served as the original photometric standard (magnitude 0), has a prominent debris disk, and was the first star other than the Sun to be photographed and have its spectrum recorded.
Struve 2398 A: A K5 red dwarf at 3.6 parsecs in Draco, one half of a wide binary system of red dwarfs. It is among the nearest stellar systems to the Sun.
Struve 2398 B: The K5 red dwarf companion to Struve 2398 A at 3.5 parsecs. Together they form one of the closest binary systems to the Solar System.
Xihe: A G8 star in Lyra at 139 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by China after the solar goddess. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Ross 154: A nearby M3.5 flare star at 3.0 parsecs in Sagittarius, one of the closest stars to the Sun. It produces frequent energetic flares despite being only 17% of the Sun's mass.
Sheliak: Beta Lyrae, the prototype of the Beta Lyrae class of semi-detached eclipsing binaries at 295 parsecs. Mass transfer between its components creates a thick accretion disk, making it one of the most studied interacting binary systems.
Ainalrami: Nu-1 Sagittarii, a K1 bright giant at 345 parsecs in Sagittarius. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the eye of the archer.'
Nunki: Sigma Sagittarii, the second brightest star in Sagittarius, a B2.5 dwarf at 70 parsecs. It has one of the oldest known star names, appearing in Babylonian records, and it marks the handle of the Teapot asterism.
Kaveh: A G8 dwarf in Serpens at 127 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Iran after the mythological blacksmith. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Alya: Theta-1 Serpentis, an A5 dwarf at 47 parsecs in the tail of Serpens Cauda. It forms a close visual pair with its twin Theta-2 Serpentis.
Alya B: Theta-2 Serpentis, the A5 companion to Alya at 52 parsecs. The pair form a beautiful matched double in small telescopes.
Sulafat: Gamma Lyrae, a B9 giant at 190 parsecs and the second brightest star in Lyra after Vega. Its name means 'the turtle' in Arabic, an early name for the constellation.
Ascella: Zeta Sagittarii, an A3 subgiant at 27 parsecs forming the base of the Teapot asterism. It is a close binary with a 21-year orbital period.
Okab: Zeta Aquilae, an A0 dwarf at 25 parsecs in the body of the Eagle. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the eagle.'
Meridiana: Alpha Coronae Australis, the brightest star in Corona Australis (the Southern Crown), an A0/A1 dwarf at 38 parsecs. Despite being the alpha star, it is only magnitude 4.1 in a faint constellation.
Albaldah: Pi Sagittarii, an F2 giant at 156 parsecs in the Teapot of Sagittarius. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the town,' referencing a star group.
Altais: Delta Draconis, a G9 giant at 30 parsecs in the body of the Dragon. It is one of the nearer giant stars visible from northern latitudes.
Aladfar: Eta Lyrae, a B2.5 subgiant at 426 parsecs in the constellation Lyra. It is the faintest of the named stars in this small but prominent constellation.
Gumala: An F8 dwarf in Sagittarius at 28 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Indonesia. It hosts one of the first known 'hot Jupiter' exoplanets.
Belel: A K0 giant in Sagittarius at 111 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Senegal. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Arkab Prior: Beta-1 Sagittarii, a B9 dwarf at 96 parsecs forming the front leg of the Archer. It is an optical pair with Arkab Posterior, the two appearing close in the sky but at different distances.
Sika: A G1 dwarf in Sagittarius at 58 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Togo. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Arkab Posterior: Beta-2 Sagittarii, an F2 giant at 41 parsecs forming the rear leg of the Archer. Despite sharing the Beta designation with Arkab Prior, it lies at a significantly different distance.
Rukbat: Alpha Sagittarii, a B8 dwarf at 56 parsecs marking the Archer's knee. Despite being the alpha star, it is only the 15th brightest star in the constellation.
Anser: Alpha Vulpeculae, an M0 giant at 91 parsecs and the brightest star in the small constellation Vulpecula the Fox. Its Latin name means 'the goose,' a relic of the constellation's original name 'Vulpecula et Anser.'
Albireo: Beta Cygni A, one of the most celebrated double stars in the sky, a K3 giant at 133 parsecs. Its striking gold-and-blue contrast with its B8 companion makes it a favorite telescopic target.
Albireo B: The B8 blue companion to Albireo at 123 parsecs, providing the spectacular color contrast with the golden primary. Whether the two form a true binary remains debated.
Uruk: An F8 dwarf in Sagitta at 118 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Iraq after the ancient Sumerian city. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Alsafi: Sigma Draconis, a K0 dwarf at just 5.8 parsecs in Draco and one of the closest solar-type stars. It has been a frequent target for SETI searches and exoplanet surveys.
Campbell's Hydrogen Star: A Wolf-Rayet star in Cygnus surrounded by a ring nebula of ejected hydrogen, one of the most visually striking planetary nebulae. Despite its 'star' designation, it is actually the central star of a nebula exhibiting powerful stellar winds.
Sham: Alpha Sagittae, a G0 bright giant at 130 parsecs and the brightest star in the tiny constellation Sagitta the Arrow. Its name means 'the arrow' in Arabic.
Fawaris: Delta Cygni, a B9.5 giant at 51 parsecs forming the west wing of Cygnus the Swan. It is a triple system where the primary will become the 'North Star' around 11,250 CE due to precession.
Tarazed: Gamma Aquilae, a K3 bright giant at 121 parsecs flanking Altair on one side. Its Persian name means 'the beam of the scale,' and its deep orange color contrasts with Altair's white.
Altair: Alpha Aquilae, the 12th brightest star in the sky at magnitude 0.76, an A7 main-sequence star at just 5.1 parsecs. One of the fastest-rotating bright stars known, it spins so rapidly that it is 14% wider at the equator than at the poles, a shape directly imaged by interferometry.
Libertas: Xi Aquilae, a K0 giant at 56 parsecs in Aquila hosting a confirmed exoplanet. Named 'freedom' in Latin through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign.
Alshain: Beta Aquilae, a G8 subgiant at 14 parsecs and the third brightest star in Aquila. Despite its Beta designation, it is notably fainter than Altair and Tarazed.
Terebellum: Omega Sagittarii, a G3/G5 giant at 26 parsecs forming part of a small quadrilateral asterism in Sagittarius. It is one of the closer named stars in this direction.
Cygnus X-1: One of the strongest X-ray sources in the sky and the first widely accepted stellar-mass black hole candidate, a B0 supergiant at 595 parsecs with an unseen companion of about 21 solar masses. Its identification as a black hole in the 1970s famously resolved a scientific bet between Stephen Hawking and Kip Thorne.
Tianfu: Tau Aquilae, a K0 giant at 142 parsecs in Aquila. Its Chinese-derived name references a historical asterism meaning 'the celestial raft.'
Antinous: Theta Aquilae, a B9.5 giant at 88 parsecs in the now-defunct constellation Antinous that was merged with Aquila. It is a spectroscopic binary.
Chechia: A G5 star in Aquila at 66 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Tunisia. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Algedi: Alpha-2 Capricorni, a G6/G8 giant at 32 parsecs forming a wide optical pair with Alpha-1. Both stars are themselves doubles, making the system appear as a wide pair to the naked eye.
Alshat: Nu Capricorni, a B9 subgiant at 78 parsecs in the constellation Capricornus. Its name means 'the sheep' in Arabic.
Dabih: Beta Capricorni, a complex multiple star system at 100 parsecs consisting of at least five components. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the lucky star of the slaughterer.'
Sadr: Gamma Cygni, a luminous F8 supergiant at 562 parsecs at the center of the Northern Cross asterism in Cygnus. It is surrounded by the Sadr region, a complex of emission nebulae including IC 1318.
Peacock: Alpha Pavonis, a B2 subgiant at 55 parsecs and the brightest star in Pavo the Peacock. Its English name was assigned by the Royal Air Force for navigational use.
Aldulfin: Epsilon Delphini, a B6 giant at 101 parsecs in the small constellation Delphinus. It forms part of the distinctive diamond-shaped asterism of the Dolphin.
Rotanev: Beta Delphini, an F5 subgiant at 31 parsecs whose name is 'Venator' spelled backward -- a prank by the astronomer Niccolao Cacciatore, whose Latinized surname is Venator.
Sualocin: Alpha Delphini, a B9 dwarf at 78 parsecs whose name is 'Nicolaus' reversed -- the companion prank to Rotanev, both inserted into the Palermo star catalog by Cacciatore in 1814.
Deneb: Alpha Cygni, the brightest star in Cygnus and one of the most luminous stars visible to the naked eye, an A2 supergiant at 433 parsecs. With roughly 200,000 times the Sun's luminosity, it forms the Summer Triangle with Vega and Altair, and marks the tail of the celestial Swan.
Aljanah: Epsilon Cygni, a K0 giant at 22 parsecs forming one wing of Cygnus the Swan. Its name means 'the wing' in Arabic.
Albali: Epsilon Aquarii, an A1 dwarf at 64 parsecs in Aquarius. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the swallower.'
Musica: 18 Delphini, a G6 giant at 75 parsecs hosting a confirmed exoplanet. Its Latin name meaning 'music' was chosen through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign.
Polaris Australis: Sigma Octantis, the southern pole star, an F0 giant at 86 parsecs. At magnitude 5.45, it is barely visible to the naked eye and far less useful for navigation than its northern counterpart.
Solaris: A K5 star in Pegasus at 48 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Kitalpha: Alpha Equulei, the brightest star in the smallest traditional constellation Equuleus the Little Horse, a G0 giant spectroscopic binary at 58 parsecs. Its name comes from a phrase meaning 'the section of the horse.'
Lacaille 8760: An M1/M2 red dwarf at 3.9 parsecs in Microscopium, the brightest red dwarf in the sky. Despite being among the Sun's nearest neighbors, it is invisible to the naked eye.
Alderamin: Alpha Cephei, an A7 subgiant at 15 parsecs that will become the North Star around 7500 CE due to precession. It is a fast rotator and one of the closer A-type stars.
Alfirk: Beta Cephei, the prototype of the Beta Cephei class of pulsating variable stars, a B2 giant at 210 parsecs. Its small, regular brightness variations over a 4.57-hour period define this important class of stellar pulsators.
Sadalsuud: Beta Aquarii, the brightest star in Aquarius, a G0 supergiant at 165 parsecs. Its name means 'the luckiest of the lucky,' reflecting ancient astrological associations with good fortune.
Bunda: Xi Aquarii, an A7 dwarf at 55 parsecs in Aquarius. Its name comes from an Aboriginal Australian word.
Samaya: An F7 dwarf in Piscis Austrinus at 108 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Sri Lanka. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Nashira: Gamma Capricorni, an A7 giant at 48 parsecs whose name means 'the bearer of good news' in Arabic. It is a suspected Delta Scuti variable.
Azelfafage: Pi-1 Cygni, a B3 subgiant at 529 parsecs in Cygnus. Its elaborate Arabic name may mean 'the horse's foot.'
