Bosona: A K0 star in Aquarius at 194 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Bosnia and Herzegovina. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Garnet Star: Mu Cephei, one of the largest and most luminous red supergiants known, an M2 supergiant at extreme distance. William Herschel named it for its deep crimson color, and as a semi-regular variable it represents a late stage of massive stellar evolution.
Enif: Epsilon Pegasi, a K2 supergiant at 211 parsecs marking the nose of the winged horse Pegasus. It is the brightest star in the constellation and occasionally displays flare-like brightenings.
Deneb Algedi: Delta Capricorni, an A5 metallic-line eclipsing binary at 12 parsecs. It is the brightest star in Capricornus and one of the nearest eclipsing binaries to the Sun.
Aldhanab: Gamma Gruis, a B8 giant at 65 parsecs and the third brightest star in Grus the Crane. Its name means 'the tail' in Arabic.
Itonda: A G2 dwarf in Grus at 46 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by the Republic of Congo. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Kurhah: Xi Cephei, an Am metallic-line star at 30 parsecs in Cepheus. It is a double star with a faint companion.
Sadalmelik: Alpha Aquarii, a G2 supergiant at 161 parsecs and the second brightest star in Aquarius. Its name means 'the lucky star of the king' in Arabic.
Alnair: Alpha Gruis, the brightest star in Grus the Crane, a B7 subgiant at 31 parsecs. Its name simply means 'the bright one' in Arabic.
Biham: Theta Pegasi, an A2 dwarf at 28 parsecs in the constellation Pegasus. Its name derives from Arabic meaning 'the young livestock.'
Ancha: Theta Aquarii, a G8 giant at 57 parsecs in Aquarius. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the hip.'
Lang-Exster: Alpha Tucanae, the brightest star in Tucana the Toucan, a K3 giant at 61 parsecs. Its somewhat unusual name derives from a historical form used in early southern hemisphere catalogs.
Sadachbia: Gamma Aquarii, an A0 dwarf at 50 parsecs in Aquarius. Its name means 'the lucky star of hidden things' in Arabic.
Lionrock: A G8 subgiant in Aquarius at 131 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Hong Kong after the Lion Rock landmark. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Kruger 60: A nearby M2 dwarf binary at 4.0 parsecs in Cepheus, one of the closest star systems to the Sun. Both components are flare stars, and the system has a very high proper motion.
Stellio: Alpha Lacertae, an A1 dwarf at 31 parsecs and the brightest star in the faint constellation Lacerta the Lizard. Its Latin name means 'the starred lizard.'
Situla: Kappa Aquarii, a K2 giant at 66 parsecs in Aquarius. Its Latin name means 'the water jar,' describing the vessel poured by the Water Bearer.
Homam: Zeta Pegasi, a B8.5 dwarf at 63 parsecs in the body of Pegasus. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the whisperer.'
Tiaki: Beta Gruis, the second brightest star in Grus, an M5 giant at 54 parsecs with a vivid red color. Its Maori name was given through the IAU initiative and relates to the concept of guardianship.
Matar: Eta Pegasi, a G2 giant at 66 parsecs in the constellation Pegasus. It is a spectroscopic binary whose name derives from the Arabic for 'the fortunate rain.'
Babcock's star: HD 215441, a remarkable A0 peculiar star in Lacerta with the strongest magnetic field of any non-degenerate star, measuring about 34 kilogauss. Discovered by Horace Babcock, it is a unique laboratory for studying extreme stellar magnetism.
Sadalbari: Mu Pegasi, an M2 giant at 33 parsecs in Pegasus. Its name means 'the lucky star of the splendid one' in Arabic.
Skat: Delta Aquarii, an A3 dwarf at 49 parsecs and the third brightest star in Aquarius. It lies near the ecliptic and the radiant of the Delta Aquariid meteor shower.
Tengshe: A K5 supergiant in Lacerta at 490 parsecs. Its Chinese name references the 'Celestial Serpent' asterism in traditional Chinese astronomy.
Helvetios: 51 Pegasi, a G5 dwarf at 16 parsecs that became famous when the first confirmed exoplanet orbiting a Sun-like star (51 Peg b, now named Dimidium) was detected around it in 1995 by Mayor and Queloz. Named after the ancient Celtic inhabitants of Switzerland.
Fomalhaut: Alpha Piscis Austrini, the 18th brightest star in the sky at magnitude 1.17, an A3 dwarf at 7.7 parsecs. Its prominent debris disk was one of the first directly imaged, and its planet candidate Fomalhaut b generated controversy as either a genuine planet or a dust cloud.
Alfarasalkamil: Omicron Andromedae, a B6 variable star at 211 parsecs in Andromeda. Its elaborate Arabic name means 'the complete horse.'
Scheat: Beta Pegasi, a semi-regular variable M2 giant at 60 parsecs forming the upper-right corner of the Great Square of Pegasus. Its diameter varies with its brightness over a period of about 43 days.
Fumalsamakah: Beta Piscium, a B6 emission-line star at 125 parsecs and the brightest star in Pisces. Its Arabic name means 'the mouth of the fish.'
Markab: Alpha Pegasi, a B9.5 giant at 41 parsecs forming the lower-right corner of the Great Square of Pegasus. It is a hot star nearing the end of its main-sequence life.
Lacaille 9352: An M2/M3 red dwarf at 3.3 parsecs in Piscis Austrinus, the fourth closest star system observable from Earth's southern hemisphere. It has the eighth-highest proper motion of all known stars.
Ebla: A K3 dwarf in Pisces at 29 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Syria after the ancient city. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Safina: 88 Aquarii, a K1 giant at 83 parsecs in Aquarius. Its Arabic-derived name references a ship or boat.
Honores: 7 Andromedae, an F0 dwarf at 25 parsecs in Andromeda. Its Latin name means 'honors,' and it is one of the brighter non-Bayer stars in the constellation.
Danfeng: An M2 red dwarf in Tucana at 26 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by China. Despite its faintness, it is notable for hosting a confirmed exoplanet.
Salm: Tau Pegasi, an A5 dwarf at 50 parsecs in the constellation Pegasus. It is a Delta Scuti variable with small rapid brightness oscillations.
Alkarab: Upsilon Pegasi, an F8 subgiant at 52 parsecs in Pegasus. Its name derives from the Arabic for 'the drinking bucket's rope.'
Veritate: 14 Andromedae, a K0 giant at 79 parsecs hosting a confirmed exoplanet. Its Latin name meaning 'truth' was chosen through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign.
Poerava: An F7 dwarf in Tucana at 55 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by French Polynesia after a black pearl. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Udkadua: Lambda Andromedae, a G8 subgiant at 26 parsecs and an RS Canum Venaticorum variable with chromospheric activity. It is one of the closer active giant stars.
Rasalnaqa: Iota Andromedae, a B8 dwarf at 153 parsecs in Andromeda. Its Arabic name means 'the head of the she-camel.'
Errai: Gamma Cephei, a K1 subgiant at 14 parsecs hosting one of the first confirmed exoplanets. Due to precession, it will become the closest bright star to the north celestial pole around 3000 CE.
Kaffalmusalsala: Kappa Andromedae, a B9 subgiant at 52 parsecs directly imaged with a super-Jupiter companion. This massive companion straddles the boundary between giant planet and brown dwarf.
Uuba: A G8 star in Sculptor at 77 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Axolotl: A G2 dwarf in Cetus at 98 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Mexico after the iconic amphibian. It hosts a confirmed exoplanet.
Castor B: The A-type secondary component of the Castor sextuple system in Gemini at 16 parsecs. Together with Castor A, it forms the bright visual double resolvable in small telescopes.
Kaewkosin: A faint M-type star in Cancer at 23 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign by Thailand. It is one of the faintest named stars at magnitude 12.3.
Maru: A white dwarf of spectral type DQ5 in Volans at 23 parsecs, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. It is one of the very few white dwarfs with a proper name.
Ross 686: A faint M4 red dwarf at 25 parsecs in Hydra, cataloged by Frank Ross. It is a companion to the also-cataloged Ross 687 in the same field.
Wolf 359: One of the nearest stars to the Sun at 2.4 parsecs in Leo, an M6 red dwarf and one of the faintest known stars. Despite its fame in popular culture, it is far too dim to see without a telescope and is a known flare star.
Alula Australis: Xi Ursae Majoris A, a G0 dwarf at 10 parsecs that was the first binary star to have its orbit calculated (by Felix Savary in 1827). Its name means 'the first leap (southern).'
Alula Australis B: The G0 dwarf companion to Xi Ursae Majoris A, forming the historic binary whose orbit was first computed. Both components are solar-type stars.
Graffias: Xi Scorpii, an F6 subgiant at 24 parsecs in Scorpius. Its Greek name means 'the crab' and it is a multiple star system.
Alrakis: Mu Draconis, a close binary of two F7 dwarfs at 23 parsecs in Draco. Both components are nearly identical, making it a challenging but rewarding telescopic target.
Guniibuu B: 36 Ophiuchi B, a K1 dwarf at 6.0 parsecs and the companion to the Guniibuu system. Together with its A component and a more distant C, it forms one of the nearest triple star systems.
Onkaria: A faint M-type red dwarf at 13 parsecs in Ophiuchus, named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. At magnitude 14.7, it is one of the faintest stars with an IAU-approved name.
EZ Aqr: A triple red dwarf system at just 3.4 parsecs in Aquarius, one of the closest stellar systems to the Sun. All three components are M5 flare stars with very low luminosity.
Ross 248: A nearby M6 red dwarf at 3.2 parsecs in Andromeda, one of the closest stars to the Sun. Around the year 36,000 CE, it will temporarily become the nearest star to our Solar System as it passes within 0.9 parsecs.
Bélénos: An IAU-named star honoring the Celtic god of light and the Sun. This exoplanet host star in Aquila was given its name through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign.
Lusitânia: Named after the ancient Roman province that covered much of modern Portugal, this exoplanet host star received its IAU name through the NameExoWorlds initiative.
Citalá: An IAU-named star whose name derives from the Nahuatl word for star, proposed by El Salvador in the NameExoWorlds campaign. It is a known exoplanet host star.
Násti: Named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign with a Sámi word meaning star. This exoplanet host star represents the astronomical heritage of the Sámi people of Northern Europe.
Añañuca: Named after a Chilean wildflower through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. This exoplanet host star carries a name that evokes the blooming deserts of northern Chile.
Bibhā: An IAU-named exoplanet host star whose name means a bright beam of light in Bengali. Named through the NameExoWorlds initiative representing Bangladesh.
Yunü: Named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign, this exoplanet host star carries a name meaning jade woman in Chinese mythology.
Hunahpú: An IAU-named star from Maya mythology, one of the Hero Twins from the Popol Vuh. This exoplanet host star was named through the NameExoWorlds initiative.
Tupã: Named after a Guaraní supreme god associated with thunder and creation. This exoplanet host star received its name through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign representing Paraguay.
Mönch: Named after one of the famous peaks in the Bernese Alps through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign representing Switzerland. This star is a known exoplanet host.
Arcalís: An IAU-named exoplanet host star named after a valley and ski area in Andorra through the NameExoWorlds initiative.
Rosalíadecastro: Named in honor of the 19th-century Galician poet Rosalía de Castro through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. This exoplanet host star represents Spain.
Sāmaya: An IAU-named exoplanet host star whose name means tranquility in Sanskrit. Named through the NameExoWorlds initiative.
Uúba: Named through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign, this exoplanet host star carries a name meaning star in the Khoisan language of Botswana.
Axólotl: Named after the iconic Mexican salamander through the IAU NameExoWorlds campaign. This exoplanet host star represents Mexico.
